    "text": "4.1.1  The principle of mathematical induction\nLet P(n) be a given statement involving the natural  number n such that\n(i)\nThe statement is true for n = 1, i.e., P(1) is true (or true for any fixed natural\nnumber) and\n(ii)\nIf the statement is true for n = k (where k is a particular but arbitrary natural\nnumber), then the statement is also true for n = k + 1, i.e, truth of P(k) implies\nthe truth of P(k + 1). Then P(n) is true for all natural numbers n.",

    "text": "Example 7  The distributive law from algebra says that for all real numbers c, a1 and\na2, we have c (a1 + a2) = ca1 + ca2.\nUse this law and mathematical induction to prove that, for all natural numbers, n ≥ 2,\nif c, a1, a2, ...,an are any real numbers, then\nc (a1 + a2 + ... + an) = ca1 + ca2 + ... + can\nSolution Let P(n) be the given statement, i.e.,\nP(n) : c (a1 + a2 + ... + an) = ca1 + ca2 + ... can for all natural numbers n ≥ 2, for c, a1,\na2, ... an ∈ R.\nWe observe that    P(2) is true since\nc(a1 + a2) = ca1 + ca2\n(by distributive law)\nAssume that P(n) is true for some natural number k, where k > 2, i.e.,\nP(k) : c (a1 + a2 + ... + ak) = ca1 + ca2 + ... + cak\nNow to prove P(k + 1) is true, we have\nP(k + 1) : c (a1 + a2 + ... + ak + ak + 1)\n= c ((a1 + a2 + ... + ak) + ak + 1)\n= c (a1 + a2 + ... + ak) + cak + 1\n(by distributive law)\n= ca1 + ca2 + ... + cak + cak + 1\nThus P(k + 1) is true, whenever P (k) is true.\nHence, by the principle of Mathematical Induction, P(n) is true for all natural\nnumbers n ≥ 2.",

    "id": "dd3e04704f144118bc27cb6bc09bffdf",
    "page": 7,
    "text": "Solution  Let P(n) be the given statement, that is,\nP(n) : 1 × 1! + 2 × 2! + 3 × 3! + ... + n × n! = (n + 1)! – 1 for all natural numbers n.\nNote that P (1) is true, since\nP (1) : 1 × 1! = 1 = 2 – 1 =  2!  – 1.\nAssume that P(n) is true for some natural number k, i.e.,\nP(k) : 1 × 1! + 2 × 2! + 3 × 3! + ... + k × k! = (k + 1)! – 1\nTo prove P (k + 1) is true, we have\nP (k + 1) : 1 × 1! + 2 × 2! + 3 × 3! + ... + k × k! + (k + 1) × (k + 1)!\n= (k + 1)! – 1 +  (k + 1)! × (k + 1)\n= (k + 1 + 1)  (k + 1)! – 1\n= (k + 2) (k + 1)! – 1 =  ((k + 2)!  – 1\nThus P (k + 1) is true, whenever P (k) is true. Therefore, by the Principle of Mathematical\nInduction, P (n) is true for all natural number n.\nExample 10  Show by the Principle of Mathematical Induction that the sum Sn of the\nn term of the series 12 + 2 × 22 + 32 + 2 × 42 + 52 + 2 × 62 ... is given  by",
